content:
# 🚨 MANDATORY: Team Memory System 🚨

A shared team memory stores critical gotchas, conventions, and debugging insights across sessions.

## REQUIRED: /recall BEFORE Starting Any Task

You MUST run /recall at the start of every task to check for relevant prior knowledge. Skipping this risks repeating known mistakes and wasting time on already-solved problems.

```
/recall "<topic or keyword relevant to your task>"
```

Examples:
- Working on Stripe integration? → /recall "Stripe"
- Fixing a database query? → /recall "database" or /recall "Drizzle"
- Debugging a test? → /recall "testing patterns"
- Working on team creation? → /recall "team" or /recall "createId"
- Touching waivers? → /recall "waiver"

**Do NOT skip this step.** Past sessions have stored critical gotchas that will save significant debugging time.

## REQUIRED: /remember After Discoveries

When you discover something useful — a gotcha, root cause, convention, or debugging insight — you MUST save it immediately with /remember. Do not wait until the end of the session.

```
/remember "<what you learned>" --category=gotcha|convention|debugging|architecture|workflow --priority=critical|moderate|ephemeral
```

## Summary

- **BEFORE work**: /recall to load relevant memories (MANDATORY)
- **DURING work**: /remember as soon as you discover something worth saving (MANDATORY)
- Failure to use team memory means the team loses knowledge and repeats mistakes.

message.content: We are testing invite logic for competitions. So in competition can invite Athletes and part of this feature is we have source allocations where you can And set a default source allocation for a competition you're inviting from as well as a per division allocation override so maybe in some cases like most division you want to only qualify the top person, but one division you want to qualify the top two people. We have a bug where it looks like our Invite page and our registration page for this invite mechanism or feature is not respecting that allocations. We have extra invites for a qualifying source and those extra invites are able to go to Stripe and claim a spot when they are not supposed to since they had the full number of spots already allocated per the division override. I need you to investigate the code paths here and see what's up https://demo.wodsmith.com/compete/2026-wodsmith-invitational/register?divisionId=slvl_inv_champ_mrx&REDACTED https://demo.wodsmith.REDACTED

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_automation_scratch_manage: Read and update the shared scratch document attached to an automation — read, str_replace (also initialises an empty scratch), read_version (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- agent-browser: Browser automation CLI for AI agents. Use when the user needs to interact with websites, including navigating pages, filling forms, clicking buttons, taking screenshots, extracting data, testing web apps, or automating any browser task. Triggers include requests to "open a website", "fill out a form", "click a button", "take a screenshot", "scrape data from a page", "test this web app", "login to a site", "automate browser actions", or any task requiring programmatic web interaction.
- seo-aeo-best-practices: SEO and AEO best practices for metadata, Open Graph, sitemaps, robots.txt, hreflang, JSON-LD structured data, EEAT, and content optimized for search engines and AI answer surfaces. Use this skill when implementing page SEO, technical SEO, schema markup, international SEO, AI-overview readiness, or improving content for Google, ChatGPT, Perplexity, and similar assistants.
- self-improving-agent: A universal self-improving agent that learns from ALL skill experiences. Uses multi-memory architecture (semantic + episodic + working) to continuously evolve the codebase. Auto-triggers on skill completion/error with hooks-based self-correction.
- web-design-guidelines: Review UI code for Web Interface Guidelines compliance. Use when asked to "review my UI", "check accessibility", "audit design", "review UX", or "check my site against best practices".
- linear: Manage issues, projects & team workflows in Linear. Use when the user wants to read, create or updates tickets in Linear.
- breadboarding: Transform a workflow description into affordance tables showing UI and Code affordances with their wiring. Use to map existing systems or design new ones from shaped parts.
- repo-autopsy: Clone a GitHub repo into a local cache and perform deep analysis using full local toolchain (rg, ast-grep, git, fd, tokei, gitleaks). Use when the user wants to inspect, explore, audit, or understand an external GitHub repository — structure, dependencies, hotspots, exports, secrets, code stats, git blame, or targeted file reads. Triggers on phrases like "autopsy", "analyze this repo", "clone and explore", "what's inside owner/repo", or any request to investigate a third-party GitHub project without adding it to the current working tree.
- adr-skill: Create and maintain Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) optimized for agentic coding workflows. Use when you need to propose, write, update, accept/reject, deprecate, or supersede an ADR; bootstrap an adr folder and index; consult existing ADRs before implementing changes; or enforce ADR conventions. This skill uses Socratic questioning to capture intent before drafting, and validates output against an agent-readiness checklist.
- breadboard-reflection: Breadboard Analysis
- stripe-best-practices: Best practices for building Stripe integrations
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- vercel-react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- vercel-composition-patterns: React composition patterns that scale. Use when refactoring components with boolean prop proliferation, building flexible component libraries, or designing reusable APIs. Triggers on tasks involving compound components, render props, context providers, or component architecture. Includes React 19 API changes.
- shaping: Use this methodology when collaboratively shaping a solution with the user - iterating on problem definition (requirements) and solution options (shapes).
- tanstack-start-server-only: TanStack Start server-only patterns using createServerOnlyFn and cloudflare:workers env access.
Use when: accessing Cloudflare environment variables (APP_URL, STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, etc.),
creating server-only utility functions, working with Stripe or other server-only APIs,
or when you need to ensure code only runs on the server and throws if called from client.
Covers createServerOnlyFn vs createServerFn differences, env access patterns, and centralized
server-only utilities.
- documentation: Centralizes Diataxis documentation framework guidance so Claude can write, review, or improve documentation following the four distinct types (tutorials, how-to guides, reference, explanation).
- athlete-docs: Documentation guidance for competition athletes and volunteers in WODsmith. Use when writing, reviewing, or improving athlete-facing documentation including registration, scheduling, workout viewing, leaderboards, check-in, and volunteer coordination.
- ralph-mode: Autonomous AI coding loop that ships features while you sleep. Use when the user wants to run Ralph, start an autonomous coding session, work overnight, or let Claude implement multiple user stories autonomously. Triggers on "ralph", "autonomous mode", "overnight coding", "autonomous loop", or requests to implement multiple stories without supervision.
- server-fn-auth: Authentication and authorization patterns for TanStack Start server functions.
Use when: adding auth to a new server function, fixing missing authorization,
checking user permissions, protecting endpoints, or implementing organizer/volunteer
access control for competitions.
- test: Primary entry point for testing guidance. Routes to appropriate test type (unit, integration, E2E) based on what you're testing. Use when asked about "test", "testing", "write tests", "TDD", or test strategy. Embeds Testing Trophy philosophy and TDD Red-Green-Refactor as core discipline.
- claim-url-lookup: Look up claim URLs for placeholder users created via manual registration. Use when needing to find the sign-up claim link for a specific email address, debug claim token issues, or verify claim tokens exist in local KV. Triggers on "claim URL", "claim token", "claim link", "placeholder user sign-up link", or requests to find the registration invite link for an email.
- competition-organizer-docs: Generate and review documentation for competition organizers using Diataxis framework. Use when writing docs for organizer workflows like creating competitions, managing divisions, scheduling heats, handling registrations, entering results, or managing volunteers/judges.
- type-safety: Fix type assertions and improve TypeScript type safety. Use when encountering 'as unknown as' casts, manual type definitions that duplicate schema types, or unclear type errors in database queries, especially with Drizzle ORM relations. Also use when verifying types
- date-timezone: Handle date and timezone conversions correctly in forms and displays. Use when working with: Date inputs, new Date(), formatDate, toLocaleDateString, getDate(), getMonth(), getFullYear(), date parsing, UTC conversion, timezone issues, dates displaying wrong day, date-only fields in forms, YYYY-MM-DD parsing. Triggers: dates off by one day, timezone bugs, form date handling, competition dates, event dates, calendar dates.
- integration-test: Guide for writing integration tests with Vitest and Testing Library. Use when testing multi-component workflows, database interactions, React components with context providers, or full user flows. Covers the Testing Trophy philosophy (integration > unit), factory patterns for test data, MSW for network mocking, async testing patterns (waitFor, findBy), and custom render with providers. Use this for tests that cross multiple modules or layers of the application.
- permissions: Guide for working with team-based permissions and authorization in the WODsmith codebase. Use when touching TEAM_PERMISSIONS constants, hasTeamPermission/requireTeamPermission functions, adding permission checks to actions or server functions, creating features requiring authorization, or ensuring client-server permission consistency.
- unit-test: Guide for writing unit tests with Vitest. Use when writing tests for service functions, pure logic, or webhook handlers. Covers TDD Red-Green-Refactor cycle, Arrange-Act-Assert pattern, anti-patterns, pure function testing, and mocking at boundaries.
- tanstack-start-boundaries: TanStack Start server/client code boundary patterns. Use when: debugging virtual module resolution errors (tanstack-start-injected-head-scripts, tanstack-start-manifest, #tanstack-router-entry), importing from @tanstack/react-start/server, using getCookie/getRequestHeaders/getRequestUrl, adding providers to router.tsx, or when Vite fails to resolve TanStack Start imports. Covers createServerFn patterns, dynamic imports, and proper placement of server-only code.
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills using TypeScript/Bun scripts (Windows-compatible). Use when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Claude's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations.
- drag-and-drop: Implement drag and drop using @atlaskit/pragmatic-drag-and-drop. Use when implementing sortable lists, reorderable items, kanban boards, or any drag-drop interactions. Covers draggable setup, drop targets, edge detection, drag previews, and critical state management patterns to avoid performance issues.
- my-pr-comments: Loads the pr review comments for the current branch.
- review: Centralizes persona-driven code reviews (Carmack, React core, Anders, React Router, Next.js, etc.) so Claude can pick or combine expert viewpoints when the user asks for a code review or perspective-specific critique. Use when user asks for code review, architecture feedback, or expert perspective on implementation.
- local-db: Handle local database schema changes with Drizzle and PlanetScale. Use when making schema changes to src/db/schema.ts, adding/modifying database tables or columns, or when asked about database migrations. Covers the push-based local development workflow and when to generate migrations.
- e2e-test: End-to-end testing with Playwright. Use when writing E2E tests, implementing page objects, setting up test fixtures, handling authentication in tests, adding visual regression testing, or configuring E2E tests for CI. Covers when to use E2E vs integration tests, critical path identification, page object pattern, test isolation, and CI considerations.
- team-memory: Store, search, and manage team memory from AI coding sessions. Use /remember to save observations (conventions, gotchas, debugging tips, architecture decisions, workflows). Use /recall to search memories by semantic query. Use /feedback to rate memory quality (helpful/harmful/irrelevant). Triggers on any request to save, recall, search, or rate team knowledge.
- alchemy-cloudflare: Alchemy IaC patterns for deploying TanStack Start apps to Cloudflare Workers with PlanetScale databases via Hyperdrive. Use when setting up new TanStack Start projects, configuring Alchemy deployments, working with PlanetScale/Drizzle migrations, local development with Cloudflare bindings, or deploying to custom domains.
- logging: Observability logging patterns for server functions in TanStack Start on Cloudflare Workers. Use when: adding logging to server functions, debugging request flows, tracking entity CRUD operations, correlating logs across requests, adding observability to new features, troubleshooting request tracing, working with PostHog/OpenTelemetry logs. Triggers: logInfo, logError, logWarning, withRequestContext, addRequestContextAttribute, logEntityCreated, request tracing, observability, correlation IDs.
- create-pr: Create a PR from current changes with PRD gif
- sync-diagrams: Store and synchronize digrams with codebase in ai/diagrams directory
- linear-project: Work on a Linear project with structured workflow
- test-pr: Review PR changes and provide comprehensive test coverage recommendations using unit, integration, and e2e testing skills
- fix-lint: /fix-lint
- swarm:inbox: Check swarm mail inbox for messages from other agents
- swarm:hive: Query and manage swarm tasks (cells)
- swarm:swarm: Decompose a task into parallel subtasks and coordinate execution
- swarm:status: Check swarm coordination status - workers, messages, reservations
- swarm:handoff: Properly end a swarm session - release reservations, sync state, generate continuation prompt
- autoresearch:autoresearch: Use when user types /autoresearch or asks to run an autonomous iteration loop against a goal/metric. Autonomous Goal-directed Iteration — modify, verify, keep/discard, repeat. Apply to ANY task with a measurable metric.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:scenario: Use when user types /autoresearch:scenario or asks to explore edge cases from a seed scenario. Scenario-driven use case generator — explores situations, edge cases, and derivative scenarios from a seed scenario using autonomous iteration.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:debug: Use when user types /autoresearch:debug or asks to hunt bugs / find root cause iteratively. Autonomous bug-hunting loop — scientific method + autoresearch iteration. Finds ALL bugs, not just one.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:learn: Use when user types /autoresearch:learn or asks to document/learn the codebase. Autonomous codebase documentation engine — scout, learn, generate/update docs with validation-fix loop.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:reason: Use when user types /autoresearch:reason or asks for adversarial generate-critique-synthesize refinement. Isolated multi-agent adversarial refinement — generate, critique, synthesize, and judge outputs through repeated rounds until convergence. Produces a lineage of evolving candidates with documented decision rationale.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:ship: Use when user types /autoresearch:ship or asks to run the 8-phase ship/deliver workflow. Universal shipping workflow — ship code, content, marketing, sales, research, or anything through a structured 8-phase workflow.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:fix: Use when user types /autoresearch:fix or asks to repair errors iteratively until zero remain. Autonomous fix loop — one fix per iteration, atomic, auto-reverted on failure.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:plan: Use when user types /autoresearch:plan or asks to turn a goal into Scope/Metric/Direction/Verify. Interactive wizard that builds the full autoresearch config from a single Goal.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:security: Use when user types /autoresearch:security or asks for STRIDE / OWASP / red-team audit. Autonomous security audit — STRIDE threat model + OWASP Top 10 + red-team with 4 adversarial personas.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:predict: Use when user types /autoresearch:predict or asks for multi-persona pre-analysis. Multi-persona swarm prediction — pre-analyze code from multiple expert perspectives using file-based knowledge representation. Zero external dependencies.
- swarm:always-on-guidance: Always-on rule-oriented guidance for claude-plugin agents. Use to align behavior, tool usage, and model-specific defaults (GPT-5.2-code vs Opus 4.5) while avoiding deprecated bd/cass references.
- swarm:swarm-coordination: Multi-agent coordination patterns for OpenCode swarm workflows. Use when work benefits from parallelization or coordination.
- autoresearch:autoresearch: Use when user types /autoresearch, /autoresearch:plan, /autoresearch:debug, /autoresearch:fix, /autoresearch:security, /autoresearch:ship, /autoresearch:scenario, /autoresearch:predict, /autoresearch:learn, or /autoresearch:reason, or mentions "autoresearch" with a goal/metric. Autonomous Goal-directed Iteration — apply Karpathy's autoresearch principles to ANY task: modify, verify, keep/discard, repeat. Supports bounded mode via Iterations: N inline config.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content:
Before starting work, run `lat search` with one or more queries describing the user's intent.
ALWAYS do this, even when the task seems straightforward — search results may reveal critical design details, protocols, or constraints.
Use `lat section` to read the full content of relevant matches.
Do not read files, write code, or run commands until you have searched.

Remember: `lat.md/` must stay in sync with the codebase. If you change code, update the relevant sections in `lat.md/` and run `lat check` before finishing.

aiTitle: Debug invite allocation override enforcement bug

input.command: lat search "competition invite source allocation division override"
input.description: Search lat.md for invite allocation docs

input.command: lat search "invite candidate registration claim spot stripe"
input.description: Search lat.md for invite candidate flow

input.command: lat search "qualifying source per-division allocation override"
input.description: Search lat.md for division override allocations

content.content:
Index updated: 1 added, 3 updated, 0 removed

## Search results for "competition invite source allocation division override":

* Section: [[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Allocations schema]] (semantic match)
  Defined in lat.md/competition-invites.md:23-28

  > ADR-0012's per-(source, championship-division) override table. Each row says "this source contributes exactly N qualifiers to this championship division"; absence of a row means "use the source default."

* Section: [[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Source details page]] (semantic match)
  Defined in lat.md/competition-invites.md:49-56

  > ADR-0012 Phase 3: per-source details route at [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/routes/compete/organizer/$competitionId/invites/sources/$sourceId.tsx]] — hosts source meta editing plus the per-(championship division) allocation override table. The Sources tab's Edit button navigates here; the Add flow keeps the existing modal.

* Section: [[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Allocations helper]] (semantic match)
  Defined in lat.md/competition-invites.md:29-36

  > [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/server/competition-invites/allocations.ts]] hosts the resolution math that turns "raw override rows + source defaults" into a per-(source, championship division) spot count. Pure where possible so callers can unit-test without DB.

* Section: [[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites]] (semantic match)
  Defined in lat.md/competition-invites.md:1-8

  > ADR-0011 ships a qualification-source → roster → invite-round system for championship competitions. Organizers declare where invitees come from, see a unified roster from those sources, and — later — send email-locked invites in waves.

* Section: [[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Roster computation]] (semantic match)
  Defined in lat.md/competition-invites.md:57-70

  > `getChampionshipRoster({ championshipId, divisionId })` in [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/server/competition-invites/roster.ts]] returns an ordered `RosterRow[]` for a championship + division. Invite-state columns are `null` in Phase 1.

## To navigate further:

* `lat section "section#id"` — show full content with outgoing/incoming refs
* `lat search "new query"` — search for something else

## Search results for "invite candidate registration claim spot stripe":

* Section: [[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Registration hand-off from claim]] (semantic match)

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/zacjones/Documents/02.Areas/wodsmith/thewodapp-2/.claude/skills/team-memory

# Team Memory

Manage a shared team memory system backed by a Cloudflare Worker with semantic search.

## Commands

### /remember — Store an observation

Save a new observation to team memory.

```bash
TEAM_MEMORY_URL=https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev bun run .claude/skills/team-memory/scripts/remember.ts "<observation text>" [--category=<category>] [--priority=<priority>]
```

**Categories:** `convention`, `gotcha`, `debugging`, `architecture`, `workflow`
**Priorities:** `critical`, `moderate`, `ephemeral`

Defaults: category=convention, priority=moderate

Examples:
```bash
TEAM_MEMORY_URL=https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev bun run .claude/skills/team-memory/scripts/remember.ts "PlanetScale returns strings for COUNT columns — always wrap with Number()" --category=gotcha --priority=critical
TEAM_MEMORY_URL=https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev bun run .claude/skills/team-memory/scripts/remember.ts "Use ULID for ID generation, not CUID2" --category=convention
```

### /recall — Search memories

Search team memory by semantic query.

```bash
TEAM_MEMORY_URL=https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev bun run .claude/skills/team-memory/scripts/recall.ts "<query>" [--limit=<n>] [--category=<category>] [--priority=<priority>]
```

Default limit: 5. Results are ranked by relevance.

Examples:
```bash
TEAM_MEMORY_URL=https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev bun run .claude/skills/team-memory/scripts/recall.ts "database migration patterns"
TEAM_MEMORY_URL=https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev bun run .claude/skills/team-memory/scripts/recall.ts "Stripe" --category=gotcha --limit=10
```

### /feedback — Rate a memory

Provide feedback on a memory's usefulness.

```bash
TEAM_MEMORY_URL=https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev bun run .claude/skills/team-memory/scripts/feedback.ts <observation-id> <signal> [--note="<reason>"]
```

**Signals:** `helpful`, `harmful`, `irrelevant`

Examples:
```bash
TEAM_MEMORY_URL=https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev bun run .claude/skills/team-memory/scripts/feedback.ts 01J5K3M2N7 helpful --note="Saved me from a production bug"
TEAM_MEMORY_URL=https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev bun run .claude/skills/team-memory/scripts/feedback.ts 01J5K3M2N7 irrelevant
```

## Environment

Production URL: `https://team-memory.zacjones93.workers.dev`

All commands below include the env var inline. If `TEAM_MEMORY_URL` is already set in your shell, the prefix is optional.

## Hooks

- **SessionStart**: Reminds the agent about the memory system and how to use /recall for task-relevant lookups
- **SessionEnd**: Reads transcript JSONL, extracts user/assistant messages, POSTs to `/sessions`
- **Export**: Syncs memories to MEMORY.md between `<!-- BEGIN TEAM-MEMORY -->` / `<!-- END TEAM-MEMORY -->` markers

content.content:
## Team Memory Results (5)

- **[01KJVPZAHQ38S75XVF6Q03PEBP]** (architecture/moderate, score: 0.08, deprecated)
  Multi-division registrations create one commerce_purchase per division sharing the same stripeCheckoutSessionId. Group by this field for invoice display.

- **[01KJ23Y0CYX1M387XM1RSJY0NC]** (architecture/moderate, score: 0.07, deprecated)
  After Stripe checkout redirect, there's a race condition: the user lands on the registered page before the Cloudflare Workflow finishes processing. Use getRegistrationPurchaseStatusFn to check for a pending purchase and show a polling UI instead of redirecting away. The function lives in competition-detail-fns.ts.

- **[01KJE8V0JY25KV7SM3PYSJRA66]** (architecture/critical, score: 0.08, deprecated)
  Placeholder user claim flow: When organizer creates manual registration for new athlete, a claim token is stored in KV (claim-token:{token}) with 90-day expiry. The registration email links to /sign-up?claim=TOKEN. Signup with valid claim token auto-verifies email. Signup without token for placeholder email requires email verification. Login is blocked for users with \!emailVerified (returns generic 'Invalid email or password' to avoid revealing account existence). Regular signups (no placeholder) are unaffected — still auto-verified.

- **[01KJ25XCWHJ8CQBYESG50VGND9]** (architecture/critical, score: 0.09, deprecated)
  inviteUserToTeamInternal in registration.ts no longer sends emails — email sending was extracted to sendPendingTeammateEmails() which queries the team_invitation table and sends competition team invite emails. Callers of registerForCompetition are responsible for calling sendPendingTeammateEmails afterward. In the Stripe checkout workflow this is a discrete step (send-teammate-invitations) to prevent duplicate emails on retry.

- **[01KJKD9MWNVY8PE4D1ZBNZFQWH]** (gotcha/critical, score: 0.10, deprecated)
  `inviteUserToTeam` auto-joins existing users immediately, bypassing the invitation flow. Volunteer/registration flows that need the acceptance form must pass `forceInvitation: true` to skip the auto-join path

[[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Allocations helper]] (lat.md/competition-invites.md:29-36)

> ## Allocations helper
>
> [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/server/competition-invites/allocations.ts]] hosts the resolution math that turns "raw override rows + source defaults" into a per-(source, championship division) spot count. Pure where possible so callers can unit-test without DB.
>
> [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/server/competition-invites/allocations.ts#resolveSourceAllocations]] is the pure function: given a source row, the championship's divisions, the source's allocation rows, and (for series sources) the comp count, it returns `{ total, byDivision }`. Algorithm: per championship division, use the override `spots` if a row exists; else use the source default — `globalSpots ?? 0` for single-comp, `(directSpotsPerComp ?? 0) * seriesCompCount + (globalSpots ?? 0)` for series, applied per division as the default. `0` overrides are honored ("this source gives this division nothing"); they do not collapse to the default.
>
> [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/server/competition-invites/allocations.ts#listAllocationsForChampionship]] is the DB read: joins `competition_invite_source_division_allocations` with `competition_invite_sources` filtered by `championshipCompetitionId`, returning the raw allocation rows for every source attached to that championship in one query. Callers (the route loader, the roster computation, the claim guardrail) feed those rows into `resolveSourceAllocations`.
>

aiTitle: Debug invite allocation override enforcement bug

content.content:
[[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Registration hand-off + Stripe workflow]] (lat.md/competition-invites.md:217-229)

> ## Registration hand-off + Stripe workflow
>
> [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/server-fns/registration-fns.ts]]'s `initiateRegistrationPaymentFn` accepts an optional `inviteToken`. When supplied:
>
> - The token is resolved + claimability-checked + email-matched against the session — any of these failing short-circuits with an error that the registration form can surface.
> - The submitted `items` must be exactly the invited division — `items.length === 1 && items[0].divisionId === invite.championshipDivisionId`. Invites are pinned per division, so the server rejects both wrong-division submissions and "invited division plus extras". The registration form locks the UI to match (single read-only division, no multi-select) when `inviteToken` is present.
> - The registration-window check is skipped for invite-holders (invites often precede public-open or survive past close). The form mirrors this — the `!registrationOpen` disable is bypassed for both the submit button and the form inputs (division select, affiliate, team fields, waiver toggles, question fields) when the invite lock is active. Otherwise the submit unlocks but the required fields stay frozen, and the invitee can't actually fill anything in.
> - The single item's purchase metadata gets `inviteId` tagged so the Stripe workflow can flip the invite on payment success.
>
> [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/workflows/stripe-checkout-workflow.ts]] reads `inviteId` from the purchase metadata inside `create-registration`, threads it through `RegistrationStepResult`, and runs a new `update-competition-invite-status` step after registration creation. The step flips the invite to `accepted_paid`, sets `paidAt`, sets `claimedRegistrationId`, and nulls `claimToken` so a replay of the original email link short-circuits. Idempotent via the `status = "pending"` guard on the update predicate so a workflow retry doesn't double-flip. `processCheckoutInline` (local-dev path) runs the same helper to keep behavior consistent without Cloudflare Workflows.
>
> When a 100%-off coupon zeroes the total, the registration server fn takes the `allFree` branch and skips Stripe entirely — so it mirrors the workflow flip inline right after creating the registration row. Without this, an invite-locked free checkout would leave the invite stuck in `pending` even though the athlete is registered.
>

## This section references:

* [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/server-fns/registration-fns.ts]] (apps/wodsmith-start/src/server-fns/registration-fns.ts)
* [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/workflows/stripe-checkout-workflow.ts]] (apps/wodsmith-start/src/workflows/stripe-checkout-workflow.ts)

commit e4b8ab14d317ec513c6182b9c27d18ce327c12b2
Author: Ian Jones <jones58ian@gmail.com>
Date:   Thu Apr 30 23:33:21 2026 -0600

    fix(invites): show all candidates + hold allocation during Stripe checkout (#440)
    
    * fix(invites): show all candidates regardless of allocation cutoff
    
    The Candidates roster sliced entries by the per-(source, championship-division)
    allocation cutoff, silently dropping eligible registrants whenever a source
    had more active registrations than the championship's allocated spots. The
    cutoff is allocation budget metadata that feeds the Sent-tab denominator,
    not a candidate filter — organizers need every eligible athlete on the page
    so they can choose whom to invite. Drop the slice; remove the now-unused
    `resolveChampionshipDivisions` helper and import path.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
    
    * feat(invites): hold allocation slot during in-flight Stripe checkout
    
    The invite-allocation guardrail counted only `accepted_paid` invites
    toward the per-(source, championshipDivision) cap. Two athletes claiming
    the last spot concurrently both passed the soft gate; the second only
    discovered the conflict at webhook time and was charged-then-refunded.
    
    Mirror the regular division-capacity pattern: a slot is also occupied
    while an athlete has a `pending` invite-driven `commerce_purchases` row
    within the 35-min Stripe-session TTL. The new `getOccupiedCountForBucket`
    sums accepted_paid invites + held pending purchases, joining purchases
    to invites via `metadata.inviteId` so only invite-driven holds count.
    The webhook re-check passes its own `purchaseId` as `excludePurchaseId`
    to avoid self-counting — same trick the existing division/competition
    caps already use. Abandoned Stripe sessions free their hold implicitly
    once the TTL filter cuts them off; no sweep job needed.
    
    Replaces `getAcceptedPaidCountForBucket` at the two call sites (claim
    landing + Stripe webhook); the original helper stays exported for
    unit-test callers that want the raw accepted-only count.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
    
    ---------
    
    Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

content.content:
[[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Invites schema]] (lat.md/competition-invites.md:103-112)

> ## Invites schema
>
> Each invite is a per-athlete row in [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/db/schemas/competition-invites.ts#competitionInvitesTable]] carrying the email-locked claim token, status, and origin attribution. ULID primary keys via [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/db/schemas/common.ts#createCompetitionInviteId]].
>
> The `activeMarker` column is the correctness pivot: it holds the literal `"active"` while `status IN (pending, accepted_paid)` and is NULL on every terminal state. Combined with the unique index `(championshipCompetitionId, email, championshipDivisionId, activeMarker)` this enforces "at most one active invite per (championship, division, email)" while letting declined/expired/revoked rows accumulate (MySQL treats multiple NULLs as distinct). The `claimToken` column is plaintext `varchar(255)` with its own unique index (`competition_invites_claim_token_idx`) — same shape as [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/db/schemas/teams.ts#teamInvitationTable]]'s `token` column, looked up directly via `eq()` rather than hashed. Terminal transitions null the token so historical rows coexist under the same multi-NULL semantics. `sendAttempt` (default 0) is the re-send counter that drives the Resend `Idempotency-Key` suffix; reusing `invite.id` across re-issues stays safe because the suffix rotates.
>
> Draft bespoke rows (created before any send) use a distinctive shape: `activeMarker = "active"`, `claimToken = NULL`, `expiresAt = NULL`, `emailDeliveryStatus = "skipped"`. The unique-active index blocks duplicate bespoke adds for the same (championship, division, email). Secondary indexes cover the organizer table queries — `(roundId)`, `(sourceId)`, `(origin)`, `(status)`, `(email)`, plus `(championshipCompetitionId)` and `(userId)` for roster-side joins.
>
> Phase 2 uses `roundId = ""` as a sentinel pending the Phase 3 `competition_invite_rounds` table; the column is NOT NULL with a default of `""` so a Phase-3 backfill can rewrite every Phase-2 row to a synthetic "Round 1 — Backfill" without schema gymnastics.
>

[[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Invites schema]] (lat.md/competition-invites.md:103-112)

> ## Invites schema
>
> Each invite is a per-athlete row in [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/db/schemas/competition-invites.ts#competitionInvitesTable]] carrying the email-locked claim token, status, and origin attribution. ULID primary keys via [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/db/schemas/common.ts#createCompetitionInviteId]].
>
> The `activeMarker` column is the correctness pivot: it holds the literal `"active"` while `status IN (pending, accepted_paid)` and is NULL on every terminal state. Combined with the unique index `(championshipCompetitionId, email, championshipDivisionId, activeMarker)` this enforces "at most one active invite per (championship, division, email)" while letting declined/expired/revoked rows accumulate (MySQL treats multiple NULLs as distinct). The `claimToken` column is plaintext `varchar(255)` with its own unique index (`competition_invites_claim_token_idx`) — same shape as [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/db/schemas/teams.ts#teamInvitationTable]]'s `token` column, looked up directly via `eq()` rather than hashed. Terminal transitions null the token so historical rows coexist under the same multi-NULL semantics. `sendAttempt` (default 0) is the re-send counter that drives the Resend `Idempotency-Key` suffix; reusing `invite.id` across re-issues stays safe because the suffix rotates.
>
> Draft bespoke rows (created before any send) use a distinctive shape: `activeMarker = "active"`, `claimToken = NULL`, `expiresAt = NULL`, `emailDeliveryStatus = "skipped"`. The unique-active index blocks duplicate bespoke adds for the same (championship, division, email). Secondary indexes cover the organizer table queries — `(roundId)`, `(sourceId)`, `(origin)`, `(status)`, `(email)`, plus `(championshipCompetitionId)` and `(userId)` for roster-side joins.
>
> Phase 2 uses `roundId = ""` as a sentinel pending the Phase 3 `competition_invite_rounds` table; the column is NOT NULL with a default of `""` so a Phase-3 backfill can rewrite every Phase-2 row to a synthetic "Round 1 — Backfill" without schema gymnastics.
>

[[lat.md/competition-invites#Competition Invites#Allocations schema]] (lat.md/competition-invites.md:23-28)

> ## Allocations schema
>
> ADR-0012's per-(source, championship-division) override table. Each row says "this source contributes exactly N qualifiers to this championship division"; absence of a row means "use the source default."
>
> Defined by [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/db/schemas/competition-invites.ts#competitionInviteSourceDivisionAllocationsTable]]. ULID primary keys via [[apps/wodsmith-start/src/db/schemas/common.ts#createCompetitionInviteSourceDivisionAllocationId]] (`cisda_*` prefix). UNIQUE index on `(sourceId, championshipDivisionId)` enforces one allocation per pair so upsert semantics are unambiguous; a secondary index on `championshipDivisionId` powers the Sent-tab denominator query that sums across sources for one division. No FKs (PlanetScale convention) — references resolve to `competition_invite_sources.id` and `competition_divisions.id` of the championship in code.
>

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Competition-invite claim resolution (Phase 2 sub-arc B).
3	 *
4	 * Read-side logic for the invite claim flow. Given a URL-bound plaintext
5	 * token, this module:
6	 * - looks up the `competition_invites` row by plaintext `claimToken`
7	 *   ({@link resolveInviteByToken}) — mirrors the `team_invitations.token`
8	 *   pattern,
9	 * - asserts the row is still claimable — active status, token still
10	 *   present, expiry in the future, competition accepting invites
11	 *   ({@link assertInviteClaimable}),
12	 * - and compares the invite's locked email against a session's email,
13	 *   returning a discriminated result the route loader branches on
14	 *   ({@link identityMatch}).
15	 *
16	 * Nothing here *transitions* invite status — the `pending → accepted_paid`
17	 * hop happens inside the Stripe webhook workflow (Phase 2 sub-arc C). This
18	 * module is side-effect-free beyond DB reads, so it's safe to call from
19	 * route loaders, server functions, and tests.
20	 *
21	 * Pure helpers (`identityMatch`, `assertInviteClaimable`, types) live in
22	 * [[./identity.ts]] so route files can import them without dragging in
23	 * `getDb` (and the `cloudflare:workers` binding) — Vite chokes on the latter
24	 * when bundling the client.
25	 */
26	// @lat: [[competition-invites#Claim resolution]]
27	
28	import { and, eq, gt, sql } from "drizzle-orm"
29	import { getDb } from "@/db"
30	import {
31	  COMMERCE_PURCHASE_STATUS,
32	  commercePurchaseTable,
33	} from "@/db/schemas/commerce"
34	import {
35	  COMPETITION_INVITE_ACTIVE_MARKER,
36	  COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS,
37	  type CompetitionInvite,
38	  competitionInvitesTable,
39	} from "@/db/schemas/competition-invites"
40	import { competitionsTable } from "@/db/schemas/competitions"
41	import { PENDING_PURCHASE_MAX_AGE_MINUTES } from "@/server-fns/competition-divisions-fns"
42	import {
43	  listAllocationsForChampionship,
44	  resolveSourceAllocations,
45	} from "./allocations"
46	import { assertInviteClaimable, InviteNotClaimableError } from "./identity"
47	import { getSourceById } from "./sources"
48	
49	// Re-export the pure helpers/types so existing imports of this module keep
50	// working. Route files should import from `./identity` directly.
51	export {
52	  assertInviteClaimable,
53	  type IdentityMatchOptions,
54	  type IdentityMatchResult,
55	  type IdentityMatchSession,
56	  type InviteClaimableError,
57	  InviteNotClaimableError,
58	  identityMatch,
59	} from "./identity"
60	
61	// ============================================================================
62	// Resolve by token
63	// ============================================================================
64	
65	/**
66	 * Look up the invite row by plaintext `claimToken`. Mirrors the
67	 * `team_invitations.token` pattern (`eq(token, ...)` direct lookup).
68	 * Returns `null` when no row exists — callers render the generic
69	 * "invite link is invalid or expired" page without leaking whether the
70	 * token ever existed.
71	 *
72	 * The `activeMarker = "active"` filter deliberately excludes
73	 * declined/expired/revoked rows: those have had their `claimToken`
74	 * nulled, but we belt-and-suspenders the query so a stale token that
75	 * somehow survived never resolves. Paid rows also have their token
76	 * nulled, so the happy path "you already claimed" short-circuit is
77	 * handled by {@link assertInviteClaimable} on the live re-read, not here.
78	 */
79	export async function resolveInviteByToken(
80	  tokenPlaintext: string,
81	): Promise<CompetitionInvite | null> {
82	  if (!tokenPlaintext) return null
83	  const db = getDb()
84	  const rows = await db
85	    .select()
86	    .from(competitionInvitesTable)
87	    .where(
88	      and(
89	        eq(competitionInvitesTable.claimToken, tokenPlaintext),
90	        eq(
91	          competitionInvitesTable.activeMarker,
92	          COMPETITION_INVITE_ACTIVE_MARKER,
93	        ),
94	      ),
95	    )
96	    .limit(1)
97	  return rows[0] ?? null
98	}
99	
100	// ============================================================================
101	// Composite lookup (convenience)
102	// ============================================================================
103	
104	export interface ResolveInviteForClaimResult {
105	  invite: CompetitionInvite
106	}
107	
108	/**
109	 * Combined resolve + claimability check. Callers that just want the
110	 * "is this a usable invite right now?" answer use this so they don't
111	 * forget one of the two steps. Throws {@link InviteNotClaimableError} on
112	 * any failure so the route loader can switch on `.reason`.
113	 */
114	export async function resolveInviteForClaim(
115	  tokenPlaintext: string,
116	  now: Date = new Date(),
117	): Promise<ResolveInviteForClaimResult> {
118	  const invite = await resolveInviteByToken(tokenPlaintext)
119	  if (!invite) {
120	    throw new InviteNotClaimableError("not_found")
121	  }
122	  assertInviteClaimable(invite, now)
123	  return { invite }
124	}
125	
126	// ============================================================================
127	// Allocation guardrail (ADR-0012 Phase 5)
128	// ============================================================================
129	
130	/**
131	 * Count `competition_invites` rows in `accepted_paid` for a single
132	 * `(sourceId, championshipDivisionId)` bucket. Used by the claim-time
133	 * allocation guardrail and the Stripe-workflow re-check.
134	 *
135	 * `Number()` wraps the count because the PlanetScale driver can return a
136	 * string for COUNT(*) — matches the pattern used elsewhere in this module.
137	 */
138	// @lat: [[competition-invites#Claim resolution]]
139	export async function getAcceptedPaidCountForBucket(args: {
140	  sourceId: string
141	  championshipDivisionId: string
142	}): Promise<number> {
143	  const db = getDb()
144	  const rows = await db
145	    .select({ count: sql<number>`count(*)` })
146	    .from(competitionInvitesTable)
147	    .where(
148	      and(
149	        eq(competitionInvitesTable.sourceId, args.sourceId),
150	        eq(
151	          competitionInvitesTable.championshipDivisionId,
152	          args.championshipDivisionId,
153	        ),
154	        eq(
155	          competitionInvitesTable.status,
156	          COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS.ACCEPTED_PAID,
157	        ),
158	      ),
159	    )
160	  return Number(rows[0]?.count ?? 0)
161	}
162	
163	/**
164	 * Count occupied slots in a `(sourceId, championshipDivisionId)` bucket.
165	 * Mirrors the regular division-capacity pattern: a slot is occupied when
166	 * an invite has been claimed (`accepted_paid`) OR an athlete is mid-Stripe-
167	 * checkout for an invite-driven registration.
168	 *
169	 * "Mid-checkout" = a `commerce_purchases` row in `pending` with
170	 * `metadata.inviteId` pointing at an invite in this bucket and
171	 * `created_at > now - PENDING_PURCHASE_MAX_AGE_MINUTES`. The TTL matches
172	 * the Stripe checkout session expiry — abandoned sessions free their hold
173	 * implicitly without a sweep job. Pass `excludePurchaseId` to skip the
174	 * webhook's own purchase row during the authoritative race-close.
175	 *
176	 * Existing accepted-paid count + soft-hold count = the number to compare
177	 * against the bucket's allocation. Two athletes claiming the last spot
178	 * concurrently both see the hold in the count, so the second one bounces
179	 * at claim load instead of paying-then-refunding.
180	 */
181	// @lat: [[competition-invites#Claim resolution]]
182	export async function getOccupiedCountForBucket(args: {
183	  sourceId: string
184	  championshipCompetitionId: string
185	  championshipDivisionId: string
186	  excludePurchaseId?: string
187	}): Promise<number> {
188	  const db = getDb()
189	  const cutoff = new Date(
190	    Date.now() - PENDING_PURCHASE_MAX_AGE_MINUTES * 60 * 1000,
191	  )
192	
193	  const [accepted, pending] = await Promise.all([
194	    db
195	      .select({ count: sql<number>`count(*)` })
196	      .from(competitionInvitesTable)
197	      .where(
198	        and(
199	          eq(competitionInvitesTable.sourceId, args.sourceId),
200	          eq(
201	            competitionInvitesTable.championshipDivisionId,
202	            args.championshipDivisionId,
203	          ),
204	          eq(
205	            competitionInvitesTable.status,
206	            COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS.ACCEPTED_PAID,
207	          ),
208	        ),
209	      ),
210	    db
211	      .select({ count: sql<number>`count(*)` })
212	      .from(commercePurchaseTable)
213	      .innerJoin(
214	        competitionInvitesTable,
215	        sql`${competitionInvitesTable.id} = JSON_UNQUOTE(JSON_EXTRACT(${commercePurchaseTable.metadata}, '$.inviteId'))`,
216	      )
217	      .where(
218	        and(
219	          eq(
220	            commercePurchaseTable.competitionId,
221	            args.championshipCompetitionId,
222	          ),
223	          eq(
224	            commercePurchaseTable.divisionId,
225	            args.championshipDivisionId,
226	          ),
227	          eq(commercePurchaseTable.status, COMMERCE_PURCHASE_STATUS.PENDING),
228	          gt(commercePurchaseTable.createdAt, cutoff),
229	          eq(competitionInvitesTable.sourceId, args.sourceId),
230	          args.excludePurchaseId
231	            ? sql`${commercePurchaseTable.id} != ${args.excludePurchaseId}`
232	            : sql`1 = 1`,
233	        ),
234	      ),
235	  ])
236	
237	  return Number(accepted[0]?.count ?? 0) + Number(pending[0]?.count ?? 0)
238	}
239	
240	/**
241	 * Resolve the per-(source, championship-division) allocation for one
242	 * invite. Returns `null` for bespoke invites (`sourceId IS NULL`) — the
243	 * allocation model doesn't apply to them.
244	 *
245	 * Uses {@link listAllocationsForChampionship} to find an explicit override;
246	 * falls back to the source default (computed via
247	 * {@link resolveSourceAllocations}) when no override row is present. Loads
248	 * the source row + series comp-count only when needed (the slow claim
249	 * path).
250	 */
251	// @lat: [[competition-invites#Claim resolution]]
252	export async function resolveAllocationForInvite(args: {
253	  invite: Pick<
254	    CompetitionInvite,
255	    "sourceId" | "championshipDivisionId" | "championshipCompetitionId"
256	  >
257	}): Promise<number | null> {
258	  if (!args.invite.sourceId) return null
259	
260	  const allocations = await listAllocationsForChampionship({
261	    championshipCompetitionId: args.invite.championshipCompetitionId,
262	  })
263	
264	  // Fast path: explicit override row for this (source, division).
265	  const override = allocations.find(
266	    (a) =>
267	      a.sourceId === args.invite.sourceId &&
268	      a.championshipDivisionId === args.invite.championshipDivisionId,
269	  )
270	  if (override) return override.spots
271	
272	  // Fallback: load the source row + (if series) its comp count, then run
273	  // the same default math the loader uses.
274	  const source = await getSourceById(args.invite.sourceId)
275	  if (!source) return null
276	
277	  let seriesCompCount: number | undefined
278	  if (source.sourceGroupId) {
279	    const db = getDb()
280	    const rows = await db
281	      .select({ count: sql<number>`count(*)` })
282	      .from(competitionsTable)
283	      .where(eq(competitionsTable.groupId, source.sourceGroupId))
284	    seriesCompCount = Number(rows[0]?.count ?? 0)
285	  }
286	
287	  const resolved = resolveSourceAllocations({
288	    source,
289	    championshipDivisions: [
290	      { id: args.invite.championshipDivisionId, label: "" },
291	    ],
292	    allocations: allocations.filter((a) => a.sourceId === args.invite.sourceId),
293	    seriesCompCount,
294	  })
295	
296	  return resolved.byDivision[args.invite.championshipDivisionId] ?? 0
297	}
298	
299	// ============================================================================
300	// Find active invite for email (used by registered-redirect cross-check)
301	// ============================================================================
302	
303	/**
304	 * Read-only helper: does an active invite exist for this
305	 * (championship, division, email) tuple? Used by the claim page to
306	 * short-circuit a second click of an *accepted_paid* link to "you're
307	 * already registered" without reissuing a token.
308	 *
309	 * Returns the first matching active row or `null`. Email normalization is
310	 * the caller's responsibility — pass already-normalized input.
311	 */
312	export async function findActiveInviteForEmail(params: {
313	  championshipCompetitionId: string
314	  championshipDivisionId: string
315	  email: string
316	}): Promise<CompetitionInvite | null> {
317	  const db = getDb()
318	  const rows = await db
319	    .select()
320	    .from(competitionInvitesTable)
321	    .where(
322	      and(
323	        eq(
324	          competitionInvitesTable.championshipCompetitionId,
325	          params.championshipCompetitionId,
326	        ),
327	        eq(
328	          competitionInvitesTable.championshipDivisionId,
329	          params.championshipDivisionId,
330	        ),
331	        eq(competitionInvitesTable.email, params.email),
332	        eq(
333	          competitionInvitesTable.activeMarker,
334	          COMPETITION_INVITE_ACTIVE_MARKER,
335	        ),
336	      ),
337	    )
338	    .limit(1)
339	  return rows[0] ?? null
340	}
341

798	        // the gate while one is mid-payment.

1	/**
2	 * Pure (no-DB) helpers for the invite claim flow.
3	 *
4	 * Split out of [[./claim.ts]] so route files can import {@link identityMatch}
5	 * + {@link assertInviteClaimable} + the type/error exports without dragging
6	 * in `getDb` (and the `cloudflare:workers` binding) — that import poisons
7	 * Vite's client bundle. The DB-using helpers stay in `claim.ts`.
8	 */
9	// @lat: [[competition-invites#Claim resolution]]
10	
11	import {
12	  COMPETITION_INVITE_ACTIVE_MARKER,
13	  COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS,
14	  type CompetitionInvite,
15	  type CompetitionInviteStatus,
16	} from "@/db/schemas/competition-invites"
17	
18	// ============================================================================
19	// Types
20	// ============================================================================
21	
22	export type InviteClaimableError =
23	  | "not_found"
24	  | "expired"
25	  | "declined"
26	  | "revoked"
27	  | "already_paid"
28	  | "over_allocated"
29	
30	/**
31	 * Result of the per-(source, championship-division) claim-time allocation
32	 * guardrail. Pure check — see {@link assertInviteWithinAllocation} for the
33	 * decision rules. The DB inputs (allocation row + accepted-paid count) are
34	 * resolved by the caller in [[./claim.ts]].
35	 */
36	export type InviteAllocationCheck =
37	  | { ok: true }
38	  | { ok: false; reason: "over_allocated" }
39	
40	export type IdentityMatchResult =
41	  | { ok: true }
42	  | { ok: false; reason: "wrong_account" | "needs_sign_in" | "needs_sign_up" }
43	
44	export interface IdentityMatchSession {
45	  /** `null` / `undefined` when the visitor is signed out. */
46	  email?: string | null
47	}
48	
49	export interface IdentityMatchOptions {
50	  /**
51	   * Whether WODsmith has a user account matching `invite.email`. The route
52	   * loader supplies this by querying `userTable` — it isn't tracked on the
53	   * invite row because a user may sign up *later*.
54	   */
55	  accountExistsForInviteEmail: boolean
56	}
57	
58	export class InviteNotClaimableError extends Error {
59	  readonly reason: InviteClaimableError
60	  constructor(reason: InviteClaimableError, message?: string) {
61	    super(message ?? `Invite is not claimable: ${reason}`)
62	    this.name = "InviteNotClaimableError"
63	    this.reason = reason
64	  }
65	}
66	
67	// ============================================================================
68	// Helpers
69	// ============================================================================
70	
71	function normalizeEmail(email: string): string {
72	  return email.trim().toLowerCase()
73	}
74	
75	// ============================================================================
76	// Claimability
77	// ============================================================================
78	
79	/**
80	 * Throws {@link InviteNotClaimableError} unless the invite is:
81	 * - status = `pending`,
82	 * - has a live `claimToken` and `expiresAt` in the future.
83	 *
84	 * `accepted_paid`, `declined`, `expired`, and `revoked` each map to a
85	 * distinct `reason` so the route can render a tailored message (and so
86	 * support queries can tell them apart from a generic "invalid").
87	 */
88	export function assertInviteClaimable(
89	  invite: CompetitionInvite,
90	  now: Date = new Date(),
91	): void {
92	  switch (invite.status as CompetitionInviteStatus) {
93	    case COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS.PENDING:
94	      break
95	    case COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS.ACCEPTED_PAID:
96	      throw new InviteNotClaimableError("already_paid")
97	    case COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS.DECLINED:
98	      throw new InviteNotClaimableError("declined")
99	    case COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS.EXPIRED:
100	      throw new InviteNotClaimableError("expired")
101	    case COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS.REVOKED:
102	      throw new InviteNotClaimableError("revoked")
103	    default:
104	      throw new InviteNotClaimableError("not_found")
105	  }
106	
107	  // Token must be live. The lookup already required a non-null token,
108	  // but guard against a race (e.g. webhook nulled the token between
109	  // resolve and assert) so we never claim a just-terminated invite.
110	  if (!invite.claimToken) {
111	    throw new InviteNotClaimableError("not_found")
112	  }
113	
114	  if (invite.expiresAt && now >= invite.expiresAt) {
115	    throw new InviteNotClaimableError("expired")
116	  }
117	
118	  if (invite.activeMarker !== COMPETITION_INVITE_ACTIVE_MARKER) {
119	    throw new InviteNotClaimableError("not_found")
120	  }
121	}
122	
123	// ============================================================================
124	// Identity match
125	// ============================================================================
126	
127	/**
128	 * Decide what happens next based on the visitor's session and whether a
129	 * WODsmith account exists for the invite's locked email.
130	 *
131	 * - signed in, session email matches invite email → `{ ok: true }`
132	 * - signed in, session email differs               → `wrong_account`
133	 * - signed out, account exists                     → `needs_sign_in`
134	 * - signed out, no account                         → `needs_sign_up`
135	 *
136	 * Case-insensitive on both sides via local lowercase normalization.
137	 */
138	export function identityMatch(
139	  session: IdentityMatchSession | null | undefined,
140	  invite: CompetitionInvite,
141	  opts: IdentityMatchOptions,
142	): IdentityMatchResult {
143	  const sessionEmail = session?.email
144	  const inviteEmail = normalizeEmail(invite.email)
145	
146	  if (sessionEmail) {
147	    if (normalizeEmail(sessionEmail) === inviteEmail) {
148	      return { ok: true }
149	    }
150	    return { ok: false, reason: "wrong_account" }
151	  }
152	
153	  if (opts.accountExistsForInviteEmail) {
154	    return { ok: false, reason: "needs_sign_in" }
155	  }
156	  return { ok: false, reason: "needs_sign_up" }
157	}
158	
159	// ============================================================================
160	// Allocation guardrail (ADR-0012 Phase 5)
161	// ============================================================================
162	
163	/**
164	 * Pure check: given the invite plus the resolved allocation for its
165	 * `(sourceId, championshipDivisionId)` and the current accepted-paid count
166	 * for that bucket, is there room for one more claim?
167	 *
168	 * Bespoke invites (`sourceId === null`) bypass — return `{ ok: true }`.
169	 *
170	 * `allocation` is the resolved spot count (already accounts for default
171	 * vs. override). `acceptedCount` is the current count of
172	 * `competition_invites` rows with `status === 'accepted_paid'` matching
173	 * `(sourceId, championshipDivisionId)`.
174	 *
175	 * `allocation === 0` is treated as "no cap configured" rather than
176	 * "cap is zero" — the organizer hasn't allocated this division to this
177	 * source, but they issued an invite anyway. Over-issue at issue time is
178	 * allowed per ADR-0012; matching that intent at claim time means we let
179	 * the athlete through. This mirrors how the Sent tab falls back to a
180	 * raw "X accepted" string when no denominator is set.
181	 *
182	 * Returns `{ ok: false, reason: "over_allocated" }` when:
183	 * - `invite.sourceId !== null` AND
184	 * - `allocation > 0` AND
185	 * - `acceptedCount >= allocation`.
186	 *
187	 * Otherwise returns `{ ok: true }`.
188	 */
189	export function assertInviteWithinAllocation(args: {
190	  invite: { sourceId: string | null }
191	  allocation: number
192	  acceptedCount: number
193	}): InviteAllocationCheck {
194	  if (args.invite.sourceId === null) return { ok: true }
195	  if (args.allocation <= 0) return { ok: true }
196	  if (args.acceptedCount >= args.allocation) {
197	    return { ok: false, reason: "over_allocated" }
198	  }
199	  return { ok: true }
200	}

/**
 * Stripe Checkout Workflow
 *
 * Cloudflare Workflow that processes checkout.session.completed events
 * asynchronously with durable steps and per-step retries.
 *
 * The webhook handler verifies the Stripe signature, dispatches to this
 * workflow (keyed by event ID for idempotency), and returns 200 immediately.
 *
 * Steps:
 * 1. create-registration: Core DB operations (idempotency checks, capacity, registration, payment)
 * 2. send-confirmation-email: Email notification (retries independently)
 * 3. send-slack-notification: Slack notification (retries independently)
 *
 * In local dev (where Workflows aren't available), the webhook handler
 * calls processCheckoutInline() which runs the same logic synchronously.
 */

import type { WorkflowEvent, WorkflowStep } from "cloudflare:workers"
import { WorkflowEntrypoint } from "cloudflare:workers"
import * as Sentry from "@sentry/cloudflare"
import { and, count, eq, gt, ne, sql } from "drizzle-orm"
import { getDb } from "@/db"
import {
  COMMERCE_PAYMENT_STATUS,
  COMMERCE_PURCHASE_STATUS,
  COMPETITION_INVITE_STATUS,
  commercePurchaseTable,
  competitionDivisionsTable,
  competitionInvitesTable,
  competitionRegistrationAnswersTable,
  competitionRegistrationsTable,
  competitionsTable,
  REGISTRATION_STATUS,
  scalingLevelsTable,
  userTable,
} from "@/db/schema"
import {
  logError,
  logInfo,
  logWarning,
} from "@/lib/logging/posthog-otel-logger"
import { getSentryOptions } from "@/lib/sentry/server"
import { notifyCompetitionRegistration } from "@/lib/slack"
import { getStripe } from "@/lib/stripe"
import {
  notifyRegistrationConfirmed,
  registerForCompetition,
} from "@/server/registration"
import { recordRedemption, cleanupStripeCoupon } from "@/server/coupons"
import {
  recordPaymentCompleted,
  recordRefundCompleted,
} from "@/server/commerce/financial-events"
import { PENDING_PURCHASE_MAX_AGE_MINUTES } from "@/server-fns/competition-divisions-fns"
import { calculateDivisionCapacity } from "@/utils/division-capacity"
import { calculateCompetitionCapacity } from "@/utils/competition-capacity"
import {
  getOccupiedCountForBucket,
  resolveAllocationForInvite,
} from "@/server/competition-invites/claim"
import { assertInviteWithinAllocation } from "@/server/competition-invites/identity"

export interface CheckoutCompletedParams {
  stripeEventId: string
  session: {
    id: string
    payment_intent: string | null
    amount_total: number | null
    customer_email: string | null
    metadata: {
      purchaseId: string
      competitionId: string
      divisionId: string
      userId: string
      couponId?: string
      stripeCouponId?: string
      couponCode?: string
      couponDiscountCents?: string
    }
  }
}

interface RegistrationStepResult {
  registrationId: string
  userId: string
  competitionId: string
  divisionId: string
  purchaseId: string
  amountTotal: number | null
  customerEmail: string | null
  userName: string | null
  competitionName: string
  divisionName: string | null
  teamName: string | null
  registrationDivisionId: string | null
  registrationTeamName: string | null
  registrationPendingTeammates: string | null
  competitionSlug: string
  competitionStartDate: string | Date | null
  userEmail: string | null
  userFirstName: string | null
  /**
   * ADR-0011 Phase 2: if the purchase metadata carried an `inviteId`,
   * the registration-creating step hands it off so a downstream step can
   * flip the invite's status to `accepted_paid`. Null when this was a
   * non-invite registration.
   */
  inviteId: string | null
}

// =========================================================================
// Standalone processing functions (used by both Workflow and inline fallback)
// =========================================================================

async function createRegistration(
  session: CheckoutCompletedParams["session"],
): Promise<RegistrationStepResult | null> {
  const db = getDb()
  const { purchaseId, competitionId, divisionId, userId } = session.metadata

  // IDEMPOTENCY CHECK 1: Get purchase and check status
  const existingPurchase = await db.query.commercePurchaseTable.findFirst({
    where: eq(commercePurchaseTable.id, purchaseId),
  })

  if (!existingPurchase) {
    logError({
      message: "[Workflow] Purchase not found",
      attributes: { purchaseId },

606	      .set({

608	        paymentStatus: COMMERCE_PAYMENT_STATUS.PAID,
609	        paidAt: new Date(),
610	      })
611	      .where(eq(competitionRegistrationsTable.id, result.registrationId))
612	
613	    // Store registration answers if present
614	    if (registrationData.answers && registrationData.answers.length > 0) {
615	      await db.insert(competitionRegistrationAnswersTable).values(
616	        registrationData.answers.map((answer) => ({
617	          questionId: answer.questionId,
618	          registrationId: result.registrationId,
619	          userId,
620	          answer: answer.answer,
621	        })),
622	      )
623	    }
624	
625	    // Mark purchase as completed
626	    await db
627	      .update(commercePurchaseTable)
628	      .set({
629	        status: COMMERCE_PURCHASE_STATUS.COMPLETED,
630	        stripePaymentIntentId: session.payment_intent ?? undefined,
631	        completedAt: new Date(),
632	      })
633	      .where(eq(commercePurchaseTable.id, purchaseId))
634	
635	    // Record payment in financial event log
636	    try {
637	      await recordPaymentCompleted({
638	        purchaseId,
639	        teamId: competition.organizingTeamId,
640	        totalCents: existingPurchase.totalCents,
641	        platformFeeCents: existingPurchase.platformFeeCents,
642	        stripeFeeCents: existingPurchase.stripeFeeCents,
643	        organizerNetCents: existingPurchase.organizerNetCents,
644	        stripePaymentIntentId: session.payment_intent ?? undefined,
645	      })
646	    } catch (eventErr) {
647	      logWarning({
648	        message:
649	          "[Workflow] Failed to record payment event (non-fatal)",
650	        error: eventErr,
651	        attributes: { purchaseId },
652	      })
653	    }
654	
655	    // Record coupon redemption if present
656	    if (session.metadata.couponId && session.metadata.stripeCouponId) {
657	      try {
658	        await recordRedemption({
659	          couponId: session.metadata.couponId,
660	          userId,
661	          purchaseId,
662	          competitionId,
663	          amountOffCents: Number(session.metadata.couponDiscountCents || 0),
664	          stripeCouponId: session.metadata.stripeCouponId,
665	        })
666	        await cleanupStripeCoupon(session.metadata.stripeCouponId)
667	      } catch (couponErr) {
668	        logWarning({
669	          message: "[Workflow] Coupon redemption/cleanup failed (non-fatal)",
670	          error: couponErr,
671	          attributes: { purchaseId, couponId: session.metadata.couponId },
672	        })
673	      }
674	    }
675	
676	    logInfo({
677	      message: "[Workflow] Registration created",
678	      attributes: {
679	        registrationId: result.registrationId,
680	        purchaseId,
681	        competitionId,
682	        userId,
683	      },
684	    })
685	
686	    // Fetch user for notifications
687	    const user = await db.query.userTable.findFirst({
688	      where: eq(userTable.id, userId),
689	    })
690	
691	    return {
692	      registrationId: result.registrationId,
693	      userId,
694	      competitionId,
695	      divisionId,
696	      purchaseId,
697	      amountTotal: session.amount_total,
698	      customerEmail: session.customer_email,
699	      userName: user
700	        ? `${user.firstName || ""} ${user.lastName || ""}`.trim() || null
701	        : null,
702	      competitionName: competition.name,
703	      divisionName: divisionRecord?.label ?? null,
704	      teamName: registrationData.teamName ?? null,
705	      registrationDivisionId: divisionId,
706	      registrationTeamName: registrationData.teamName ?? null,
707	      registrationPendingTeammates: null,
708	      competitionSlug: competition.slug,
709	      competitionStartDate: competition.startDate,
710	      userEmail: user?.email ?? null,
711	      userFirstName: user?.firstName ?? null,
712	      inviteId: registrationData.inviteId ?? null,
713	    }
714	  } catch (err) {
715	    logError({
716	      message: "[Workflow] Failed to create registration",
717	      error: err,
718	      attributes: { purchaseId, competitionId, userId },
719	    })
720	    await db
721	      .update(commercePurchaseTable)
722	      .set({ status: COMMERCE_PURCHASE_STATUS.FAILED })
723	      .where(eq(commercePurchaseTable.id, purchaseId))
724	    // Re-throw to trigger step retry
725	    throw err
726	  }
727	}

733	 * Idempotent: guarded by status=pending so a duplicate workflow run

736	 */
737	async function updateCompetitionInviteStatus(

765	  const affected =

767	    (updateResult as unknown as [{ affectedRows?: number }])[0]?.affectedRows ??
768	    0
